objects.name: CyberNetSec.io
objects.description: CyberNetSec.io is a cybersecurity intelligence platform providing timely, accurate, deduplicated, and actionable threat intelligence for security professionals. The platform aggregates, deduplicates, and enriches threat data from diverse public sources, mapping all threats to MITRE ATT&CK and D3FEND frameworks with operationally-focused analysis, detection guidance, and mitigation recommendations. Operated by security professionals, for the security community.

objects.name: Jason Gomes
objects.description: Cybersecurity practitioner and founder of CyberNetSec.io with over 10 years of specialized experience in security operations, threat intelligence, incident response, and security automation. Expertise spans SOAR/XSOAR orchestration, threat intelligence platforms, SIEM/UEBA analytics, and cyber fusion centers.

objects.name: Nightspire
objects.description: A ransomware group operating a Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) model that uses double extortion tactics. Claimed responsibility for the attack on Pat S.r.l.
goals: Financial extortion of victim organizations through data encryption and the threat of leaking exfiltrated data.

objects.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
objects.description: Used by the Nightspire group to steal sensitive data from the victim's network before encryption, as part of their double extortion strategy.

objects.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
objects.description: Used by the Nightspire group to encrypt files on the victim's network to cause operational disruption and extort a ransom.

objects.name: Nightspire
objects.description: The ransomware payload used by the Nightspire group to encrypt victim files as part of their double extortion attacks.

objects.name: Nightspire Leak Site
objects.description: The dark web data leak site operated by the Nightspire group to publish names of victims and threaten the release of exfiltrated data.

objects.name: AdFind Execution for Reconnaissance
objects.description: Detects the execution of AdFind, a legitimate command-line tool for querying Active Directory, often used by attackers for network reconnaissance as suggested in the hunting hints.

objects.name: AdFind Execution for Reconnaissance
objects.description: Detects the execution of AdFind, a legitimate command-line tool for querying Active Directory, often used by attackers for network reconnaissance as suggested in the hunting hints.
objects.pattern:
title: AdFind Execution for Reconnaissance
id: 9c5b1a3d-8e4f-4b1a-9c8d-6f5e4d3c2b1a
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of AdFind, a tool often used by adversaries for Active Directory reconnaissance.
references:
  - https://www.dexpose.io/blog/nightspire-targets-pat-s-r-l-in-confirmed-ransomware-incident
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-25
tags:
  - attack.t1018
  - attack.discovery
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\adfind.exe'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Mimikatz Execution for Credential Dumping
objects.description: Detects the execution of Mimikatz, a well-known credential dumping tool, as suggested in the hunting hints for pre-ransomware activity.

objects.name: Mimikatz Execution for Credential Dumping
objects.description: Detects the execution of Mimikatz, a well-known credential dumping tool, as suggested in the hunting hints for pre-ransomware activity.
objects.pattern:
title: Mimikatz Execution for Credential Dumping
id: 1e8d7c6b-5a4f-4e3d-8c2b-1a9f8e7d6c5b
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of Mimikatz, a tool widely used for credential dumping.
references:
  - https://www.dexpose.io/blog/nightspire-targets-pat-s-r-l-in-confirmed-ransomware-incident
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-25
tags:
  - attack.t1003
  - attack.credential_access
level: critical
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\mimikatz.exe'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Domain Admin Group Discovery via Net Command
objects.description: Detects the use of the 'net' command to enumerate privileged accounts in a domain, a key step in privilege escalation, as suggested in the hunting hints.
objects.pattern: [process:command_line = 'group' OR process:command_line = 'Domain Admins' OR process:command_line = 'net.exe' OR process:command_line = 'domain' OR process:command_line = 'net1.exe']

objects.name: Domain Admin Group Discovery via Net Command
objects.description: Detects the use of the 'net' command to enumerate privileged accounts in a domain, a key step in privilege escalation, as suggested in the hunting hints.
objects.pattern:
title: Domain Admin Group Discovery via Net Command
id: 7d6c5b4a-3e2d-4c1b-9a8f-5e4d3c2b1a9f
status: experimental
description: Detects the use of the 'net' command to enumerate members of the 'Domain Admins' group, a common reconnaissance technique.
references:
  - https://www.dexpose.io/blog/nightspire-targets-pat-s-r-l-in-confirmed-ransomware-incident
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-25
tags:
  - attack.t1069.002
  - attack.discovery
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\net.exe'
      - '\net1.exe'
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - 'group'
      - 'Domain Admins'
      - '/domain'
  condition: selection

objects.name: PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass
objects.description: Detects the use of PowerShell with an execution policy bypass, a common technique for running unsigned or malicious scripts, as suggested in the hunting hints.
objects.pattern: [process:command_line = '-ExecutionPolicy Bypass' OR process:command_line = '-exec bypass' OR process:command_line = 'powershell.exe']

objects.name: PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass
objects.description: Detects the use of PowerShell with an execution policy bypass, a common technique for running unsigned or malicious scripts, as suggested in the hunting hints.
objects.pattern:
title: PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass
id: 3c2b1a9f-8e7d-4c6b-9a5f-1e8d7c6b5a4f
status: experimental
description: Detects PowerShell being launched with the execution policy bypass flag, which can be used to run malicious scripts.
references:
  - https://www.dexpose.io/blog/nightspire-targets-pat-s-r-l-in-confirmed-ransomware-incident
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-25
tags:
  - attack.t1059.001
  - attack.execution
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
    CommandLine|contains:
      - ' -exec bypass'
      - ' -ExecutionPolicy Bypass'
  condition: selection

objects.name: Nightspire Ransomware Group Claims Attack on Italian Firm Pat**** S.r.l
objects.description:
## Executive Summary
On May 24, 2026, the ransomware operator known as **Nightspire** claimed a new victim, adding an Italian company named **Pat**** S.r.l** to its data leak site. This public posting is a standard tactic in the double extortion ransomware model, designed to pressure the victim into paying a ransom. While specific details about the attack vector and the scope of the compromise are not yet public, the group's statement, "Data is not available now," strongly implies that they have exfiltrated data and are threatening to release it. This incident is another example of the persistent and global threat posed by ransomware gangs targeting businesses of all sizes.

---

## Threat Overview
**Nightspire** is a ransomware group that operates a Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) model. Like many modern ransomware gangs, it employs a double extortion strategy. This involves:

1.  **Data Exfiltration**: Before encrypting files, the attackers steal sensitive data from the victim's network ([`T1041 - Exfiltration Over C2 Channel`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1041/)).
2.  **Data Encryption**: The attackers deploy their ransomware payload to encrypt files across the network, causing operational disruption ([`T1486 - Data Encrypted for Impact`](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1486/)).
3.  **Extortion**: The group then demands a ransom payment in exchange for a decryption key and a promise to delete the stolen data.

The cryptic message "Data is not available now" is a threat that the data will be made available (i.e., leaked) if the victim does not comply with their demands. The attack on **Pat**** S.r.l** follows this well-established pattern.

---

## Impact Assessment
For **Pat**** S.r.l**, the potential impact is multifaceted. The encryption of their systems could lead to significant business disruption, halting operations and causing financial losses. The threat of a data leak introduces additional risks, including reputational damage, loss of customer trust, and potential regulatory fines under GDPR for failing to protect personal data. The public nature of the claim on **Nightspire**'s leak site immediately puts the company under pressure from customers, partners, and regulators.

---

## IOCs — Directly from Articles
No specific Indicators of Compromise were provided in the source articles.

---

## Cyber Observables — Hunting Hints
To detect activity associated with ransomware groups like **Nightspire**, security teams should hunt for pre-ransomware indicators:

| Type | Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `process_name` | `adfind.exe` | A legitimate command-line tool for querying Active Directory, often used by attackers for network reconnaissance. |
| `process_name` | `mimikatz.exe` | A well-known credential dumping tool used to harvest passwords and hashes from memory. |
| `command_line_pattern` | `net group "Domain Admins" /domain` | A command used to enumerate privileged accounts in a domain, a key step in privilege escalation. |
| `log_source` | Antivirus/EDR logs | Alerts for the disabling of security products are a major red flag that often precedes ransomware deployment. |

---

## Detection & Response
Organizations facing a similar attack should immediately activate their incident response plan.

1.  **Isolate and Contain**: Disconnect affected machines from the network to stop the ransomware from spreading. Isolate critical systems and backups.
2.  **Assess the Breach**: Begin an investigation to understand the scope of the compromise. It is critical to determine which systems were affected, what data was exfiltrated, and how the attackers gained initial access.
3.  **Engage Experts**: Contact incident response professionals and legal counsel specializing in ransomware to help navigate the technical and legal complexities of the situation.
4.  **Preserve Evidence**: Collect and preserve logs, disk images, and other forensic artifacts that can aid in the investigation.

---

## Mitigation
General best practices for defending against ransomware include:

1.  **MFA and Strong Passwords**: Enforce Multi-Factor Authentication on all external access points and for all privileged accounts. See [`M1032 - Multi-factor Authentication`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1032/).
2.  **Backup and Recovery**: Maintain a robust backup strategy with offline, immutable copies of critical data. Regularly test your ability to restore from these backups. This is the most effective defense against the encryption portion of the attack. See [`M0916 - Data Backup`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/ics/M0916/).
3.  **Network Segmentation**: Segment your network to make it harder for attackers to move laterally from a compromised workstation to critical servers. See [`M1030 - Network Segmentation`](https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/M1030/).
4.  **Security Awareness Training**: Train users to recognize and report phishing emails, which remain a common initial access vector for ransomware.
